title: Husky Farmhouse Table
summary: A farmhouse table built around four purchased turned legs — 2x4 aprons trimmed in 1x2, a Kreg-jigged 2x6 top belt-sanded flat, no leg joinery to cut.
description:
This table swaps the usual four hand-built legs for purchased dining table legs — the source uses Osborne Wood's Husky style, but any similarly-sized purchased leg works — which removes the single trickiest part of a farmhouse table build and replaces it with a bolt-on part. What's left is straightforward: an apron box trimmed with 1x2 for a layered look, and a top glued and pocket-screwed from seven 2x6 boards.

Drill every pocket hole in the aprons before assembly, including one every 8" or so facing upward — those are what attach the top later, and they're much easier to drill on a flat, unassembled apron than after the base is built. When the legs go on, double-check the pocket holes are facing up before you drive a single screw; it's a mistake worth checking for deliberately; a purchased leg installed backward can't be un-drilled.

Getting the top flat is the real skill test here: the source is blunt about it — start with straight boards, because a crooked board fights every clamp you own during the glue-up. Once assembled, belt-sand the top flat and fill any remaining gaps with food-safe silicone before it becomes a dining surface that sees regular use. That combination — sanded flat, gaps sealed — is what makes a planked farmhouse top functional for a table that will actually get used daily rather than admired occasionally.

tools.note: Flattening the planked top after glue-up.
Long bar clamps for pulling the top boards tight.

materials.name: Purchased dining table legs (e.g. Osborne Wood Husky-style)
materials.note: The source used Osborne Wood's Husky leg; any similarly-sized purchased leg substitutes.

materials.name: 2x6, 72" long
materials.note: Tabletop boards. Tongue-and-groove 2x6 is an easier-finishing alternative.

materials.name: Food-safe silicone sealant
materials.note: Sealing gaps in the planked top.

steps.title: Drill the apron pocket holes
steps.body: Drill all the pocket holes in the aprons first — two per apron end for the corner joints, plus one every 8" or so facing upward along the top edge for attaching the tabletop later. When you attach the legs, double-check the pocket holes point upward before driving screws — a leg attached with them facing the wrong way can't be un-drilled.

steps.title: Build the apron box and add legs
steps.body: Join the side and end aprons into a box with pocket screws and glue, then attach the four purchased legs to the corners.
materials: Purchased dining table legs (e.g. Osborne Wood Husky-style)

steps.title: Add the apron trim
steps.body: Attach the 1x2 trim around the underside of the apron for a layered, decorative look. This step is purely decorative — a table saw owner could instead groove the aprons directly for the same effect. Corner cross supports can be added here if extra rigidity is wanted, though the base is typically stiff enough without them.

steps.title: Glue up the top
steps.body: Start with straight boards — a crooked board fights every clamp during glue-up. Join the seven 2x6 tabletop boards edge to edge with pocket holes, glue, and long bar clamps pulled tight across the width.

steps.title: Flatten and seal the top
steps.body: Once cured, trim any uneven ends square with a circular saw, then belt-sand the top flat. Fill any remaining gaps between boards with food-safe silicone to keep food and liquid from working into the cracks.

steps.title: Attach the top and finish
steps.body: Flip the tabletop face-down, set the base on top so the pre-drilled pocket holes face the tabletop, and drive screws up through them into the top. Flip the assembled table over and finish.

images.alt: Farmhouse table with purchased turned legs and a planked 2x6 top
